meta.support: Found a translation mistake? Report it on GitHub
meta.metaDescription: Online role dealer for Mafia played over video calls. Deal role cards to players in Zoom, Meet, or Teams. Free, no registration required.
meta.metaKeywords: mafia game, online mafia, role cards, party game, team building game, video call game, zoom game, meet game

common.title: Mafia (Werewolf)
common.rolesNote: The Commissar role is always included. The number of Mafia (including the Don) is calculated automatically: one Mafia for every 3.5 players.
common.footerHint: Click a card to view it enlarged. Click again or press Esc to close. Viewed cards appear dimmed.
common.alertTooManyRoles: Error: too many roles selected ({roles}) for {players} players. Reduce the number of optional roles or increase the player count.
common.siteDesc: Online role card dealer for Mafia — deal and reveal roles to players during a video call. Works in any conference room where the webcam order stays fixed.

peaceful.name: Civilian
peaceful.desc: Civilian role. Votes during the day. The goal is to identify and eliminate the Mafia and the Maniac. Even if the Mafia are eliminated, the game continues while the Maniac is still alive.

mafia.name: Mafia
mafia.desc: Traitor. At night, chooses a victim together with the Don. During the day blends in with the civilians.

don.name: Don
don.desc: Traitor. Leader of the Mafia. At night checks one player — the host tells whether that player is the Commissar (or Patrol) or not.

commissar.name: Commissar
commissar.desc: Civilian role. At night checks one player — the host tells whether they are Mafia or not. If the Commissar dies, the role passes to the Patrol (if that role is in play and the Patrol is alive).

patrol.name: Patrol
patrol.desc: Civilian role. Wakes up with the Commissar and sees the check. At night protects one player: if the Mafia target that player, the Patrol dies instead. If the Commissar dies, the Patrol becomes the new Commissar and no longer protects.

doctor.name: Doctor
doctor.desc: Civilian role. Heals one player each night (can heal self). Prevents a Mafia shot. The same player cannot be healed two nights in a row or more than twice during the game, including self. If the Maniac is part of the session, the Doctor also checks one player each night for being the Maniac — the host shows a yes/no reaction card. If the Doctor dies, the role passes to the Nurse (if that role is in play and the Nurse is alive).

nurse.name: Nurse
nurse.desc: Civilian role. Wakes up with the Doctor and sees their choice. If the Doctor dies, the Nurse becomes the Doctor, including the Maniac check if that role is in the game. Cannot heal self; the same 'no twice in a row' and 'no more than twice per game per player' rules apply.

maniac.name: Maniac
maniac.desc: Independent role. Each night kills one player, except self. Immune to the Escort's ability. If the Maniac attacks the Escort or the player she spent the night with, both die. Wins if left one-on-one with any other player.

escort.name: Escort
escort.desc: Civilian role. Wakes up first each night. Spends the night with one player: that player's night action is blocked, and a Mafia shot aimed at them fails. If she spends the night with a Mafia member, the Mafia make no kill, but the Don still performs his check. If she spends the night with the Don, the Don makes no check, but the Mafia still perform their kill. Has no effect on the Maniac — if the Maniac attacks, both die.

jester.name: Jester
jester.desc: Civilian role that does not wake up at night. The goal is to be voted out by civilians during the day. If the Jester is voted out, they win immediately. If killed at night, they are simply eliminated without winning.

journalist.name: Journalist
journalist.desc: Civilian role. Wakes up at night before the Maniac. Points to two players: the host tells whether they belong to the same faction (both civilians or both Mafia) without specifying which one.

paragraphs: Players gather in a shared online room that supports screen sharing and webcams. Webcams are required for everyone except the host: instead of a camera, the host shares their screen with this tool. A player without a camera becomes a silent observer and cannot speak or vote. The game relies on trust: no hints or spoilers in private messages — play fair in the main call. For dealing roles by webcam order, it helps if the participant grid looks the same for everyone. Services like VDO.ninja, Butter, Whereby, Zoom, and similar conferencing tools work well.

The ideal number of players is 8 to 24. Fewer than eight is usually not very interesting, and more than twenty-four becomes too chaotic and can stretch the game for hours. Optional roles should match the group size: Patrol and Nurse are usually skipped with fewer than 15 players, and Journalist or Jester with fewer than 20. That said, the final balance can always be adjusted to suit your group.

To set the mood, the first player may tell a short story explaining where everyone is and why the group gathered. For example: the team decided to take a company trip to Sicily — and that is where our game takes place. This introduction happens on the first day and can be given a little extra time.

Everyone closes their eyes — using a mask, their hands, or a sheet of paper. The important part is honesty and no peeking. The host deals roles following the webcam order on screen — ideally the participant grid looks the same for everyone. For experienced groups, there is another option: the host calls each player in turn, the player says their number, and the host silently shows their role card. After the deal, groups meet each other: the Mafia and the Don wake up, recognize each other, and go back to sleep; then the Commissar and Patrol wake up and go back to sleep; then the Doctor and Nurse wake up and go back to sleep.
Players open their eyes and introduce themselves. There is no vote on the first day — this is a fun social Mafia game, not a competitive tournament.

Everyone closes their eyes. The host wakes the roles one by one: Escort → Mafia & Don → Commissar & Patrol → Doctor & Nurse → Journalist → Maniac. Each player shows their choice to the host using fingers or a prepared card with a large number visible on webcam. For roles that receive information (Commissar, Don, Doctor, Journalist), the host responds with a reaction card: yes or no.

The host announces the results of the night. Optionally, a player killed during the night may leave a last will — either a “soft” version (30 seconds for a final opinion) or a “hardcore” version (5 seconds for a last message). After that, there may be an optional open discussion of 1–3 minutes by prior agreement. Then players take turns nominating someone by saying the number or nickname of the chosen player. Each player has exactly one minute — miss your chance and your turn is skipped. Voting is done by raising hands when the host calls each nominee in turn. The player with the most votes is eliminated. If nobody was nominated, the day ends without elimination and the game moves to night.

Interrupting another player outside of open discussion is not allowed and results in a foul. Three fouls: the player loses their speaking turn and may only nominate without explanation. Four fouls: the player is removed from the table without a vote. A player who opens their eyes at the wrong time during the night is considered killed by the Mafia. Night events not covered by the rules must not be discussed during the day — doing so results in a foul. Gesturing while “asleep” at night is also a foul. With a large table (usually more than 12 players), additional fouls may be given for exceeding the speaking time.

Civilian victory — all Mafia and the Maniac are eliminated. Mafia victory — the number of Mafia equals or exceeds the number of civilians (the Maniac counts as a civilian). Maniac victory — the Maniac is left one-on-one with any other player. Jester victory — the Jester is nominated and voted out by civilians on any day.
hostParagraphs: Preparation

Before the game begins, make sure that all players have their webcams enabled and clearly visible. Players should sit so that both their face and hands are visible in the frame. This is necessary to monitor rule compliance during the night phase. A player without a camera may only participate as a silent observer without speaking or voting rights.

Check the order of players in the video conference grid. If the order appears differently for participants, agree in advance on how roles will be dealt (for example, by the numbering announced by the host).
Before the game starts:

• announce the player numbers;
  • make sure each player remembers their number;
  • prepare response cards for informational roles (“yes” / “no”);
  • prepare background music for the night phases if needed;
  • check that screen sharing works correctly.
<hr>
Dealing roles

During the first night, deal the roles strictly according to the previously agreed player order. Show the card so that only the specific player can see it. Give a few seconds to read the role — this is especially important for newcomers.
After the roles are dealt, introduce the groups that must know each other:

1. mafia and don
  2. commissar and patrol
  3. doctor and nurse
Each group opens their eyes only for the duration of the introduction.
<hr>
Night phase
Wake the roles in the same order every night — this helps players stay oriented:

1. escort
  2. mafia and don
  3. commissar and patrol
  4. doctor and nurse
  5. journalist
  6. maniac
Players show their choice using fingers or a card with the player's number. The host records the actions and must not give any non-verbal hints.
<hr>
Day phase
Announce only the facts:

• who was killed during the night;
  • if no one died — announce it.
Do not comment on the events or give hints to the players. The host must remain neutral.
Keep track of timing:

• player speech — up to 1 minute;
  • discussion — by team agreement;
  • voting — sequentially for each nominated player.
<hr>
Rule enforcement
Watch for violations:

• interrupting another player's speech;
  • discussing night events a player could not know about;
  • gestures or hints during the night;
  • opening eyes or peeking.
Issue fouls according to the rules. At large tables it is especially important to control speech timing.
<hr>
Main principle
The host is both the referee and the director of the game. Their task is to maintain the pace, enforce the rules, and not influence player decisions.
Clear and calm hosting makes the game enjoyable for everyone.
